content.short_summary: Sitagliptin is a DPP-4 inhibitor that may be used to help manage Type 2 diabetes as part of an individual care plan.
why_it_is_used: Sitagliptin may be prescribed alone or with other medicines to help manage Type 2 diabetes.
A healthcare team considers a person’s medical history and treatment goals when discussing this option.
how_it_works: Sitagliptin helps increase the activity of incretin hormones in the body.
When blood glucose is elevated, those hormone signals can support insulin release and reduce glucagon release.
common_benefits: May help lower blood glucose when it is prescribed for Type 2 diabetes.
It may be used with other diabetes medicines when a clinician determines that combination is appropriate.
common_side_effects: Side effects can occur and should be reviewed with a pharmacist or healthcare professional.
Serious warnings and an individual’s medical history are important parts of a prescribing discussion.
important_considerations: Research-backed editorial draft. Not yet reviewed by a qualified clinician. Must not be published.
Sitagliptin is not inherently better or worse than other options; treatment choice is individual.
Important warnings, other medicines, and health conditions should be reviewed with a qualified healthcare professional.
Medication choices are individual. This is general education, not a reason to start, stop, or change a medicine based on this app. Urgent or severe symptoms need professional care.
questions_for_healthcare_team: What is sitagliptin intended to help with in my care plan?
Which side effects or warnings should I understand before discussing this option?
How do my other medicines and health conditions affect this choice?
content.key_takeaway: Sitagliptin is a DPP-4 inhibitor used in Type 2 diabetes care; a healthcare team can explain whether it fits an individual plan.
